rationale: The citing paper gives Liechty, Ramaswamy, and Cohen (2001) a distinct role in the introduction as prior work that models consumer product choices in mass customization. It then uses that citation to define its own research gap, arguing that prior work does not explain how alternative customization configurations affect utility via product utility and perceived complexity. Although the cited paper is not reused methodologically in the empirical model, it meaningfully informs the citing paper's framing and contribution positioning.
relation_description: The citing paper builds from Liechty et al. (2001) as a key prior study on mass-customization choice modeling, then extends the line of inquiry to configuration-level evaluation (utility versus complexity) rather than only modeling chosen product options.
